topic: Berlin U-Bahn rant — stuck at Kottbusser Tor 40 minutes

turns.content: i have been standing at kottbusser tor for 40 minutes. forty. vier-null. the display just says 'Störung' which is german for 'lol good luck'
Forty minutes is brutal. Is there any indication of what the disruption actually is, or just the classic unhelpful "service disruption" with no details?

the announcement said something about a 'technische Störung' at Hermannplatz which could mean literally anything from 'a signal is broken' to 'someone looked at the infrastructure wrong and it gave up.' the BVG twitter account posted a meme about it which is helpful

The BVG social media strategy of responding to service failures with memes is... a choice. "We can't run trains on time but we have great engagement metrics" is quite the organizational priority.

Have they suggested any alternative routes, or is it just vibes and memes?

they suggested taking the bus replacement service which — and i cannot stress this enough — does not exist. there is no bus. i walked to where the bus should be and there's just a sign and some confused tourists. Quatsch. absolute Quatsch

The phantom bus replacement service is a Berlin classic. They announce it like it's a real thing that exists in the physical world, and then you get to the stop and it's just an aspirational concept. At this point it's practically performance art.

Are there other options? Could you take a different line and transfer, or is this the only viable route?

i could walk. it's like 25 minutes to where i'm going which is honestly faster than whatever the BVG is going to deliver at this point. but it's also february in berlin and it's approximately minus everything degrees outside. also the display updated once to say a train was coming in 3 minutes — that was 22 minutes ago. the 3 minute lie. the BVG's signature move. the display is just a random number generator at this point. completely decoupled from reality. eventual consistency but the eventual never arrives

"Eventual consistency but the eventual never arrives" — painfully accurate. The BVG arrival displays operate on a fundamentally different model of time than the rest of the universe.

At this point the walk is the rational choice. You've already sunk 40 minutes into waiting. The train display has proven itself to be fiction. Even if a train comes in the next 5 minutes, you've already lost the time advantage over walking. February Berlin wind is brutal, but at least you'll be moving forward instead of standing still trusting a screen that lies.

you're right. sunk cost fallacy is keeping me on this platform. i'm leaving. if a train passes me while i'm walking i will simply pass away

bvg should have an SLA. if uptime drops below 90% we all get free monthly tickets. they'd never agree to it because they know they'd be bankrupt in a week

A BVG SLA would be incredible. "We guarantee 99.9% uptime" — immediate bankruptcy. They'd have to redefine "uptime" to include "a train existed somewhere in Berlin today" just to stay solvent.

Go walk. Stay warm. And if a train does pass you, just remember: it would have stopped at every station for 4 minutes each anyway, so you're probably still arriving at the same time.

update: i'm walking. it's freezing. my fingers are so cold i can barely type this. and guess what just happened. a train went past. on the elevated track. right above me. i watched it go by like a man watching his ex at a party

Of course it did. The BVG has an internal sensor that detects when passengers leave the platform and dispatches a train within 90 seconds. It's the only system of theirs that actually works reliably.

At least you're in motion now. The next one would have been 25 minutes away anyway, probably.

the bvg app now says service is restored and trains are running 'mit Einschränkungen' which means 'badly but we're pretending it's fine.' also it says estimated wait at kotti is now 4 minutes. four. i waited forty and left and now it's four. this is targeted harassment

"Mit Einschränkungen" is doing incredible heavy lifting as a phrase. It could mean anything from "one train is slightly delayed" to "the entire network is held together with tape and optimism."

And the 4-minute wait showing up the moment you leave is just the universe maintaining its sense of comedic timing. You were the load-bearing passenger — the system couldn't recover until you stopped observing it. Schrödinger's U-Bahn.

Schrödinger's U-Bahn is genuinely the best description of the bvg i've ever heard. the train both exists and doesn't exist until you leave the platform. i'm going to bring this up at every dinner party for the rest of my life

ok i can see my destination now. made it in 20 minutes on foot. the bvg would have taken 40+ minutes of waiting plus the actual ride. walking won. public transit lost. this city

Walking: 20 minutes, predictable, functional. BVG: 40 minutes of standing in the cold trusting a display that generates fiction, followed by a train ride of unknown duration. The math was never close.

At least you got a good story and a new physics metaphor out of it. Warm up, get some feeling back in your fingers, and maybe file a complaint that the BVG will respond to with a meme.
